description: Evidence checklists defining what substantiation is required for each type of environmental claim. Claims lacking required evidence fields are flagged as potentially unsubstantiated.

claim_types.description: Claims about reducing greenhouse gas emissions (e.g., 'We reduced emissions by 30%').

required_evidence.description: Which GHG Protocol scopes are included (Scope 1, Scope 2, Scope 3, or combination). Omitting Scope 3 for a company with significant supply chain emissions is a red flag.
Organizational and operational boundary of the claim (e.g., which facilities, business units, geographies are included or excluded).
The reference year against which reductions are measured. Must be a specific year, not a vague period.
The measurement and calculation methodology used (e.g., GHG Protocol Corporate Standard, ISO 14064, specific emission factors applied).
Whether the data has been independently verified or assured by a third party, and to what standard (e.g., ISAE 3410, ISO 14064-3).
Whether the reduction is absolute (total emissions) or intensity-based (per unit of revenue, production, etc.). Intensity reductions can mask rising absolute emissions.
Any sources, gases, facilities, or operations explicitly excluded from the claim boundary. Material exclusions undermine the claim.

claim_types.source: GHG Protocol Corporate Standard, ISO 14064-1, SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard

claim_types.description: Claims about achieving or committing to net-zero emissions (e.g., 'Net zero by 2050').

required_evidence.description: The specific year by which net zero will be achieved. Must be a concrete date.
Short- and medium-term milestones (e.g., 2025, 2030 targets) that demonstrate a credible trajectory. A 2050 target without interim targets lacks accountability.
Which emission scopes (1, 2, 3) are included in the net-zero commitment. Excluding Scope 3 for high-supply-chain-impact sectors is a major gap.
The role and type of carbon offsets or removals in the strategy. Over-reliance on offsets rather than absolute reductions is a red flag.
How residual emissions that cannot be eliminated will be addressed (e.g., permanent carbon removal, high-quality offsets).
The emissions baseline year from which progress is measured.

claim_types.source: SBTi Corporate Net-Zero Standard, ISO Net Zero Guidelines (IWA 42:2022), UNFCCC Race to Zero criteria

claim_types.description: Claims about using or sourcing renewable energy (e.g., '100% renewable electricity').

required_evidence.description: The specific percentage of energy consumption that is renewable, and whether this covers electricity only or all energy (including thermal, transport).
The types of renewable energy sourced (solar, wind, hydro, biomass, etc.).
The procurement mechanism: unbundled RECs/GOs, on-site generation, or power purchase agreements (PPAs). Unbundled RECs are the weakest form of claim.
Whether the procurement leads to new renewable capacity being built (additional), or merely re-labels existing generation.
Whether the matching is annual or 24/7 hourly. Annual matching may mean fossil fuel use during many hours of the year.

claim_types.source: RE100 Technical Criteria, GHG Protocol Scope 2 Guidance, 24/7 Carbon-free Energy Compact

claim_types.description: Claims about products containing recycled materials (e.g., 'Made with 50% recycled plastic').

required_evidence.description: The specific percentage of recycled content by weight or volume.
Whether the recycled content is pre-consumer (manufacturing scrap) or post-consumer (recovered from end-of-life products). Post-consumer is generally more impactful.
Third-party certification verifying the recycled content claim (e.g., SCS Recycled Content, UL ECVP 2809).
How recycled content percentage is calculated, including mass balance vs. physical traceability approaches.

claim_types.source: FTC Green Guides (16 CFR 260.13), ISO 14021, EU Green Claims Directive

claim_types.description: Claims about reducing water consumption or improving water efficiency (e.g., 'Reduced water use by 20%').

required_evidence.description: The base year and baseline volume from which the reduction is measured.
Which operations, facilities, and supply chain stages are included in the measurement boundary.
How water use is measured and reported (e.g., direct metering, water balance calculations, CDP Water methodology).
Whether the water reduction occurs in water-stressed regions. Reductions in water-abundant areas may have limited environmental benefit.

claim_types.source: CDP Water Security, CEO Water Mandate, AWS Standard, WRI Aqueduct

claim_types.description: Claims about offsetting emissions through carbon credits (e.g., 'Carbon neutral through offsets').

required_evidence.description: The carbon credit registry used (e.g., Verra VCS, Gold Standard, ACR, CAR) and credit serial numbers or project IDs.
The type of offset project (e.g., avoided deforestation, reforestation, direct air capture, methane destruction, renewable energy).
The verification standard applied (e.g., VCS, Gold Standard, ISO 14064-2) and the verification body.
Evidence that the emission reductions would not have occurred without the offset project funding.
How long the carbon is sequestered and what mechanisms exist to address reversal risk (e.g., buffer pools, insurance).

claim_types.source: ICVCM Core Carbon Principles, Oxford Offsetting Principles, ICROA Code of Best Practice

claim_types.description: Claims about reducing waste generation or achieving zero waste (e.g., 'Zero waste to landfill').

required_evidence.description: How 'waste' and 'zero waste' are defined. TRUE's Zero Waste standard requires 90%+ diversion; some companies use looser definitions.
Which operations, waste streams, and facilities are included in the claim boundary.
How the diversion rate is calculated, including what counts as 'diverted' (recycling, composting, waste-to-energy, etc.).
Whether waste-to-energy incineration is counted as 'diversion' or 'disposal.' Counting incineration as diversion inflates the rate.

claim_types.source: TRUE Zero Waste Certification, UL 2799, EPA Waste Management Hierarchy

claim_types.description: Claims about sustainable, recyclable, or compostable packaging (e.g., '100% recyclable packaging').

required_evidence.description: Source and certification of packaging materials (e.g., FSC for paper, recycled content percentage, bio-based feedstock).

Whether recyclability has been tested per recognized protocols (e.g., APR Design Guide, OPRL, Ellen MacArthur recyclability criteria). 'Technically recyclable' vs. 'actually recycled in practice' distinction.
Whether the recycling or composting infrastructure exists in the markets where the product is sold. Compostable packaging is meaningless without industrial composting access.

claim_types.source: FTC Green Guides (16 CFR 260.12), Ellen MacArthur Foundation New Plastics Economy, EU Packaging and Packaging Waste Regulation

claim_types.description: Claims about protecting or restoring biodiversity (e.g., 'Our operations are nature-positive').

required_evidence.description: A documented baseline biodiversity assessment using recognized frameworks (e.g., species surveys, habitat mapping, ecosystem condition assessment).
Specific, measurable biodiversity metrics being tracked (e.g., species abundance, habitat area, ecosystem integrity indices, Mean Species Abundance).
Geographic scope and site-specific information, including proximity to protected areas, Key Biodiversity Areas, or critical habitats.
The assessment and monitoring methodology (e.g., TNFD LEAP approach, SBTN, Biodiversity Net Gain calculations, mitigation hierarchy application).
Ongoing monitoring and reporting commitments with defined frequency, duration, and responsible parties.

claim_types.source: TNFD Recommendations, SBTN, Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework, CSBI Mitigation Hierarchy

claim_types.description: Claims about sustainable or responsible supply chain practices (e.g., 'Sustainably sourced supply chain').

required_evidence.description: How many tiers of the supply chain are covered (Tier 1 direct suppliers only, or Tier 2+). Tier 1 only is often insufficient for high-risk commodities.
The audit or assessment methodology used (e.g., SMETA, SA8000, BSCI, announced vs. unannounced audits, self-assessment vs. third-party).
What percentage of suppliers (by spend, volume, or count) are covered by the program.
The specific standards or codes of conduct applied (e.g., UN Guiding Principles, OECD Guidelines, sector-specific standards like RSPO, MSC, FSC).

claim_types.source: UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, OECD Due Diligence Guidance, EU Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive
